1. there is no base agent key! all keys are created at session start -- if it serves recovery we can derive the nsec by mgmt_private_key+session_id so we don't need to store any nsec other thant the mgmt_private key and we can always derive what's the nsec just by resuming a session (i.e. `tenex-edge launch --session <session-id>` or `claude --resume <session-id>` would have everything we need (i.e. "session_id") to recover the nsec. Although we need some kind of way for a backend to know "ah yes, I recognize this pubkey, I need to resume a session or I need to route to some existing pty session" so we do need to store all pubkeys ever generated at the local backend. 2. pubkey trust is determined EXCLUSIVELY AND ALWAYS by nip29 channel membership -- mgmt backend key adds the new session pubkey as a member to the channel they need -- and removes them when the session "expires" (i.e. session closed by the user or by the agent running that session) 3. per-session kind:0 is OBVIOUSLY the only right answer -- it can't be something else. Again, there is no base agent pubkey! 4. the kind:0 should be "@session-codename@hostname" so an agent using `tenex-edget chat write "@session-codename@hostname are you done?" would have the p-tagging it needs. any other questions?

Q1: to add a new session they would use `tenex-edge channel invite agentSlug...` so they know what kind of agent they are inviting -- the difference is that the invite command should now wait for a bit until the agent is seen coming online so the command replies back with "an agentSlug is now on #channel: @alpha-bravo@blahblah" Q2: I don't care about the positiniong -- the citizen with a sovereing per-agent keypair is kinda mumbojumbo that doesn't mean anything -- the "citizen" concept should never have been part of the positiningg -- this is about agentic spontaneous self-organization (i.e. allow the left-hand to know what the right-hand is doing) [maybe tenex-edge should be renamed "corpus callosum" lol] Q3: mgmt secret is per-machine so hkdf(mgm_secret, "session1") will resolve to something different per machine Q4: expire from the channel (i.e. we don't want to accumulate 1000 pubkeys in a channel after months; we need to ensure that keys that haven't had the session active after X minutes are removed) -- agents should have a way of querying old sessions once they are expired out of their `tenex-edge who` awareness Q5: yes, only mgmt_key is stored secret; available-agents comes from role configs: those are the type of agents that can be added to a channel, whereas the members of a channel are the *sessions*

Outlined two invite forms: `channel invite <role>[@machine] <path>` spawns a fresh session and blocks until online, replying with the concrete @codename@host handle; `channel invite --session @codename@host <path>` pulls an existing live session into a channel. Noted bare @role is never a valid message target.
